column14:
I’m most excited about AI’s ability to streamline project/task development and help bring clarity to complex ideas more efficiently. It’s been especially valuable for refining messaging, generating strong first drafts and adapting content for different audiences without losing tone or intent. Overall, having AI as a tool has allowed me to be 10x more productive and saving time on repetitive or time-consuming tasks. 

I also see a lot of potential in building more customized tools and workflows, like GPTs tailored to specific needs, which could save time and create more consistency across our work.

column13:
One of the biggest challenges I’m feeling right now is the shift in how our department approaches AI. It’s starting to feel more like a “sport mode” environment, where there’s a lot of emphasis on calling out AI use in meetings or creating custom GPTs. At times, it comes across as more performative than practical.

With that said, evaluating our performance in AI feels very subjective, and I don’t think we fully hit the mark by adding it to evaluations on a 1–5 scale. I use AI every day and it genuinely helps me work more efficiently and effectively in my role, but it often feels like that’s not enough. I don’t have as many complex or highly visible use cases as others, and there’s a sense that those with more intricate applications are seen as “better” at AI. That can be frustrating, because it doesn’t always reflect the real impact of how AI is being used.

Because I’m not a “teacher” of AI or one of those leading these efforts, I’m evaluated as a “satisfactory” user, which doesn’t fully reflect the value I bring. Do we all need to be teachers or visionaries in AI to be considered strong performers, especially in roles where AI is just one part of the work?

I really appreciated the pace we followed in the past. It felt intentional, steady and effective, while already miles ahead of the rest of the organization. We were making real progress without it feeling forced. 

I do see the value of AI and have embraced it in my day-to-day work. At the same time, there’s only so much my role requires me to rely on it. I’d really like to see an approach that recognizes effective AI use can look different depending on the role, rather than expecting the same level or type of use from everyone.
column14: Efficiency

column13:
Adoption remains a key challenge. While there is strong encouragement to use AI to transform workflows and create new solutions, actual usage across teams is still inconsistent. This limits the overall impact of the tools being developed.

There is also a disconnect between how AI is positioned and what is practically possible. On one hand, teams are encouraged to use AI to innovate and rethink processes. On the other, corporate guardrails and limitations on deployment make it difficult to fully implement or scale these solutions. As a result, efforts to build AI-driven tools can sometimes take significant time without delivering the expected efficiencies.

Additionally, developing GPTs and new workflows requires a meaningful time investment, which can be difficult to balance alongside existing responsibilities.

Moving forward, a key opportunity is to better align expectations with capabilities—either by enabling more flexibility within current guardrails or by clearly defining how teams can effectively use AI within them. This would help drive stronger adoption and ensure AI delivers on its intended value.
column14: I see tremendous potential for AI to transform how we work—especially in building tools, improving efficiency, and rethinking existing workflows. It opens the door to more streamlined processes and new ways of solving problems. I’m excited about the direction we’re heading and the opportunities AI creates to drive meaningful change across our work.

column13: While I feel like AI has been beneficial for me as an individual contributor, the biggest challenge is that AI has not consistently created efficiencies at a team or departmental level. In some cases, it introduces delays due to lack of alignment on tools and the need for additional validation. I have also found that AI-generated outputs can lack context or strategic alignment but are still being sent out, which requires extra time to review, correct, and communicate, offsetting any potential time savings. Finally, I have found that AI performs better with structured inputs, which would require our department rethink or rebuild existing workflows to use it more effectively.
